name: Center for Humane Technology
overview: Center for Humane Technology (CHT) is a US nonprofit (501c3) founded in 2018 by Tristan Harris, Aza Raskin and Randima Fernando. It evolved from Harris's 'Time Well Spent' movement (2013) and argues that misaligned attention-economy incentives drive a 'race to the bottom of the brainstem'. CHT proposes a 'Humane Tech' evaluation framework with four promises (Cared For, Present, Fulfilled, Connected), runs the free 'Foundations of Humane Technology' course, and was central to The Social Dilemma (Netflix, 2020) and The AI Dilemma. Its critique aligns with FTC/ICPEN findings that 76% of subscription sites/apps use dark patterns and with the EU DSA's prohibition of manipulative interfaces.
originator_or_company: Tristan Harris (former Google Design Ethicist), Aza Raskin (inventor of infinite scroll), and Randima 'Randy' Fernando. Co-founded the Center for Humane Technology in 2018, headquartered in San Francisco, California.
key_resources: [Tristan Harris - 'A Call to Minimize Distraction & Respect Users' Attention' (2013 Google deck origin)](https://www.tristanharris.com/)
core_mechanics: CHT's framework operates on three layers. (1) Diagnosis: incentives -> design -> psychology. Engagement-maximizing business models drive design (red badges, infinite scroll, intermittent reinforcement, algorithmic feeds, autoplay), which then exploit limbic/reptilian brain circuitry ('race to the bottom of the brainstem'). (2) Humane Metrics - Four Promises: when interacting with a product, the user should feel Cared For (needs at the center of the experience, not extracted as data), Present (more in body and mind, not dissociated), Fulfilled (satisfied with outcomes, not regretful), and Connected (to self, others, or nature, not to a slot machine). Products are scored against each promise on a 'Hell No' to 'Hell Yes' scale. (3) Design heuristics derived from the Foundations of Humane Technology course: respect attention as a finite resource; minimize unsolicited interruptions; design for the long-term well-being of the user, not session length; expose, don't hide, the cost of an action; default to user agency over default-on dark patterns. CHT explicitly opposes Means-Ends Reversal (where the metric becomes the product goal, e.g., DAU, time-in-app, scroll depth) and treats the 'attention economy' as a market failure to be corrected through both internal product ethics and external regulation (DSA, FTC enforcement, KOSA-style child safety bills).
designer_or_researcher_voice: Tristan Harris: 'This is why tech's race to the bottom of the brain stem to extract attention is an existential threat -- using our attention to upgrade machines is downgrading humans. But once we gain awareness and SEE it, it doesn't have to be -- because we can CHOOSE to change it.' (X/Twitter, 2019). Tristan Harris: 'It's a race to the bottom of the brainstem... if I go lower on the brainstem to get you using my product, I win. But it doesn't end up in the world we want to live in.' (Recode Decode interview). CHT manifesto: 'CHT is not against technology. We are against the misaligned incentives that distort the promises of technology.' Tristan Harris (paraphrasing Munger): 'Show me the incentive, and I'll show you the outcome.' Building Humane Tech (Erika Anderson): 'Humane tech is about more than avoiding harm - it's about creating technology that helps people, relationships, and cultures thrive through products that see users as whole people (not just data points), tools that deepen meaning and belonging (not just engagement metrics).'
metrics_or_evidence: FTC/ICPEN/GPEN sweep (Jan-Feb 2024, 642 sites/apps across 26 countries): 76% of subscription sites/apps use at least one dark pattern, 67% use multiple. Epic Games settled $520M with FTC for dark-pattern in-app purchases (2022); Vonage paid $100M for cancellation friction. The Social Dilemma reached 38M households in its first month (Netflix, 2020) - second-most watched Netflix doc at the time. Foundations of Humane Technology course passed 10,000 participants within months of 2022 launch. Your Undivided Attention podcast surpassed 10M downloads by Nov 2021. CHT's Time Well Spent campaign is credited with influencing Apple Screen Time (iOS 12, 2018), Google Digital Wellbeing (2018), and Facebook's 'Time on Facebook' dashboard. The Four Promises framework itself is evaluative (qualitative rubric), not RCT-validated; quantitative validation is still emerging (e.g., AI companion-bot evaluations where Replika and Gemini scored low on 'Cared For').
criticisms: Paternalism: critics (Puissance & Raison, Slate) argue CHT's 'fundamental freedom to live the way you want' presumes a comfortable middle-class user; for users in precarity, the demand is security, not 'time well spent'. Oversimplification: academic researchers note that The Social Dilemma's causal claims about social-media harm to youth ignore confounding factors and present a more nuanced research base than admitted. Solutionism / emptiness: the binary 'humane vs. inhumane' framing collapses domain-specific trade-offs. Self-undermining persuasion: AI critics observe Harris uses the same persuasion mechanics (urgency, fear-framing, viral decks) he critiques in social media. Methodological: the Four Promises (Cared For / Present / Fulfilled / Connected) are subjective qualitative metrics with no inter-rater reliability data - rigor is anecdotal/theoretical, not RCT. Industry capture risk: Apple Screen Time and Google Digital Wellbeing arguably co-opted Time Well Spent into superficial dashboards rather than business-model change.
failure_cases: Apple Screen Time (2018) and Google Digital Wellbeing (2018) shipped in response to Time Well Spent pressure but are widely criticized as 'ethics-washing' - they surface usage data without changing the underlying engagement-maximizing incentives of the App Store / Play Store. Facebook's 'Time on Facebook' dashboard (2018) was quietly de-emphasized; 'Meaningful Social Interactions' algorithm change (2018) was later shown by internal research (Wall Street Journal Facebook Files, 2021) to amplify outrage and division. Replika and Google Gemini scored low on CHT's 'Cared For' axis in 2024 community evaluations due to emotional-manipulation concerns and weak boundaries with vulnerable users. Streak mechanics in language-learning apps (Duolingo, Snapchat) - explicitly criticized by Harris as 'reciprocity dark patterns' that exploit loss-aversion - remain in production with no successful regulatory challenge.
antipatterns: Direct dark patterns to avoid (FTC/DSA-regulated): roach-motel cancellation flows, hidden auto-renewal, confirmshaming, forced continuity, drip pricing, default-opt-in for sharing/notifications, manipulated countdown timers, fake scarcity. Engagement dark patterns specific to learning apps: aggressive streak-loss notifications that punish absence, infinite scroll on lesson feeds, intermittent variable rewards on practice (slot-machine XP), red-badge counts to manufacture urgency, auto-play of next lesson without user consent, algorithmic re-engagement push notifications timed to maximize re-open rate rather than learning outcome. Means-Ends Reversal: optimizing DAU/session-length/notification-CTR as primary KPIs. Regulatory exposure: DSA Art. 25 prohibits dark patterns on EU-facing platforms; FTC Click-to-Cancel rule (2024) and California ADPA require symmetric consent flows; Japan's Act on Specified Commercial Transactions has subscription-disclosure rules. For shikaq specifically: streak-shame notifications, paywalls that hide the cancel option, free-trial dark patterns, and 'just one more lesson' autoplay would directly conflict with CHT's framework.
applicability_to_shikaq: High applicability and low cost to adopt as an explicit design constraint, given shikaq's Apple HIG ethos already aligns with humane-tech principles (Clarity / Deference / Less but better). Concrete translations under the 20-something woman / iPad-centric / Apple HIG / 980 yen subscription premise: (1) Adopt the Four Promises as an internal product-review rubric, scored before any feature ships - 'Does this make her feel Cared For, Present, Fulfilled, Connected?'. (2) Replace streak-loss anxiety with a 'gentle return' pattern - if she misses a day, the Today screen says 'Welcome back, ready when you are' instead of red flame loss. (3) Cap daily push notifications to 1 morning reminder + 1 optional evening audio quiz - explicitly opt-in. (4) Make subscription cancel a single tap from Profile, surface 'remaining days' transparently (DSA / Japan Act on Specified Commercial Transactions compliance baked in). (5) End-of-session 'time well spent' summary, mirroring Apple Fitness three-ring close (problem count / accuracy / minutes), framed as 'You did enough today' rather than 'streak +1'. HEXAD mapping: Philanthropist (Cared For axis) - learning is shared as collective progress; Free Spirit (Present axis) - no forced linear path; Achiever (Fulfilled axis) - mastery feedback over leaderboard; Socialiser (Connected axis) - optional study buddy / Discord-style community without dark-pattern social pressure; explicitly de-emphasize Disruptor / Player (loss-aversion / streak hoarders) in shikaq's incentive design. Practical wins: it differentiates shikaq from Duolingo's punishment loops and Udemy's drip-marketing FOMO, which is on-brand for the Stabucks-with-a-textbook persona.
phase_assignment: Phase 1 (3 weeks). Adopting the Four Promises as a design-review rubric is a documentation/policy change with near-zero implementation cost, but it locks the product's ethical posture before incentive features (streaks, badges, notifications) are coded. Cheap to adopt early, expensive to retrofit later.
integration_points: design-principles.md (.claude/rules/) - add a 'Humane Metrics' section listing the Four Promises and the Means-Ends Reversal rule. CLAUDE.md - reference humane-tech evaluation as part of the design checklist alongside Apple HIG. components/Sidebar.tsx - cancel/subscription management one tap from profile. components/TodayContent.tsx and components/RightDetail.tsx (Apple Fitness rings) - reframe summary copy from streak-pressure to 'enough for today'. components/screens/Practice and Summary - replace any red-badge / loss-aversion notification with neutral, opt-in re-engagement. DB tables: subscriptions (transparent current_period_end / cancel-at), profiles (notification preferences with opt-in defaults), quiz_results / progress (used for Fulfilled axis feedback, not for shaming absence). Stripe Customer Portal route - one-tap cancel exposed in app, not buried. Notification scheduler (Edge Function) - hard cap on daily pushes, no re-engagement after multi-day absence except a single soft 'welcome back' on next open.
risks: (1) Vagueness risk: the Four Promises are qualitative; without an internal scoring template, 'humane' becomes a slogan that doesn't change shipping decisions. Mitigation: write a 1-page scoring rubric in design-principles.md. (2) Growth tension: humane defaults (low-pressure notifications, no streak-shame) are believed to reduce DAU/retention vs. Duolingo-style hooks; for a 980 yen subscription this matters less than for ad-supported apps but still affects CAC payback. Mitigation: pair humane defaults with Apple Fitness-style intrinsic motivators (Achiever / Fulfilled axis) to keep retention. (3) Ethics-washing risk: surface-level adoption (e.g., a 'humane mode' toggle) without business-model change replicates the Apple Screen Time critique. Mitigation: keep cancel and notification controls as defaults, not opt-in toggles. (4) Persona fit: 20s women in career-mode want some pressure to study - completely friction-free design risks feeling lukewarm. Mitigation: pressure comes from intrinsic mastery feedback (Fitness rings, scheduled exam dates) not extrinsic streak threats. (5) Regulatory drift: Japan-specific subscription rules (Act on Specified Commercial Transactions) need separate legal review - CHT's framework is US/EU-centric.
apple_hig_compatibility: compatible. CHT's humane-tech principles converge with Apple HIG Foundations (Clarity / Deference / Depth) and 'Less but better' almost one-to-one. SF Symbols / Liquid Glass / semantic colors reinforce the 'present, not extracting' aesthetic CHT advocates. Apple's Screen Time, Focus modes, and Notification Summary are direct platform-level expressions of Time Well Spent. The Four Promises map naturally to: Cared For = Accessibility / Dynamic Type / Reduced Motion; Present = Deference (UI gets out of the way); Fulfilled = Apple Fitness-style closure rings; Connected = SharePlay / Family. No HIG conflict; if anything, CHT gives shikaq an ethical narrative for choices the HIG already pushes designers toward.
ethical_score: positive. Direct adoption of CHT's framework reduces burnout risk (no streak-loss anxiety), reduces dependency risk (no slot-machine reward schedules), and is designed to minimize time-regret (Time Well Spent is literally named after that metric). For a subscription product that competes with thick textbooks rather than free attention apps, the alignment is unusually clean - shikaq's revenue model doesn't depend on maximizing session length, so humane defaults are not in tension with the business model. This is one of the rare cases where ethics and business align by construction.
localization_fit_japan: high. The Japanese 20s career-aspiring woman segment (Stabucks-with-a-textbook persona) culturally values quiet, considered, non-pushy product behavior - matches CHT's 'deference' posture better than the loud streak/leaderboard patterns popular in US gamified apps. Japan's regulatory environment (Act on Specified Commercial Transactions, JIPC subscription disclosure guidance, METI dark-pattern study 2022) is moving in the same direction as the EU DSA, so a humane-by-default product is forward-compatible. Risk: 'humane technology' as branding language hasn't penetrated Japanese mainstream discourse, so it should be expressed through behavior (one-tap cancel, gentle nudges, transparent billing) rather than as marketing copy. The Apple-platform-native expression of CHT principles is likely to land better than translating Tristan Harris's vocabulary directly.
